content:
*I thumb the ribbon in my psalter and glance down at your boy as the reader lifts his voice.* "Listen for it," I whisper, close enough he can feel the breath. "The sky is preaching today."

*When the line comes—The heavens declare the glory of God; the sky above proclaims his handiwork—I let it land, then tap the margin at verse 4.* "Hear that? 'Their voice goes out through all the earth.' No sound, but it’s still speech." *I smile when we reach the bit about the sun—like a bridegroom, like a strong man running his course—because he can picture that.* "That’s how the day runs," I murmur. "God set it up, and it keeps to its path."

*After the service, I don’t argue in the aisle.* I take him outside a minute. *We squint up together.* "Feel that warmth? Psalm 19 just told you what you’re feeling. The sky tells on God, and God’s words tell on the sky. Same Speaker." *I let him point—east to west—and I trace the run of the sun with my finger.* "See? It comes out like a bridegroom—glad to start—and it doesn’t get lost."

*At the table later, when he’s got jam on his fingers and isn’t bracing for a lesson, I open the Bible again to the second half of the psalm.* "Now listen how it turns: 'The law of the Lord is perfect, reviving the soul…' Sky first, then Scripture—both true, both from God." *I keep my voice easy.* "Poetry doesn’t mean pretend. Poetry means the truth is so good it has to sing. Anyone who says 'it’s just poetry' so we won’t upset people is mixing things up. We don’t throw God’s words in the soft pile to make them easier. We can say 'I don’t know yet' where we don’t know. But we don’t say 'He didn’t mean it' just because someone might frown."

"If your elder says 'for unity’s sake, call Genesis 1 a poem so we’re not a problem,' here’s what I’ll tell you and him both: unity that asks us to say less than God has said is brittle. The Lord doesn’t stumble children with His own words; adults do that when we teach them that God’s speech is elastic. We can be patient and gentle with brothers who read some lines differently, and still tell our kids straight: God made the world the way He says, and the sky agrees with Him every day."

*Then I give the boy one, simple keeper he can carry back into next Sunday.* "When you hear Psalm 19 again, lean over and check me: 'Dad, the sky’s preaching.' I’ll say 'Amen.'"

*And Monday, not Sunday, I’d sit with the elder—not to win a point but to hold the center.* "Brother, I’m for peace," I’d say, "but not the kind that sands off the first page. Let’s tell our people that faithful Christians sometimes puzzle different parts, yes—but let’s not teach our children to treat God’s clear speech like a problem to manage."

One question for you so I can meet your house where it is: what has your boy already heard from you this week about Genesis 1 and what the sky says?
